evidence.title: Treatment of Vertigo | AAFP
evidence.snippet: "> Medication Dosage Sedation Antiemesis Pregnancy category Meclizine* (Antivert) 12.5 to 50 mg orally every 4 to 8 hours + + + B Dimenhydrinate* (Dramamine) 25 to 100 mg orally, IM, or IV every 4 to 8 hours + + + B Diazepam (Valium) 2 to 10 mg orally or IV every 4 to 8 hours + + + D Lorazepam (Ativan) 0.5 to 2 mg orally, IM, or IV every 4 to 8 hours + + + D Metoclopramide (Reglan) 5 to 10 mg orally every 6 hours + + + + B 5 to 10 mg by slow IV every 6 hours Prochlorperazine (Compazine) 5 to 10 mg orally or IM every 6 to 8 hours + + + + C 25 mg rectally every 12 hours 5 to 10 mg by slow IV over 2 minutes Promethazine (Phenergan) 12.5 to 25 mg orally, IM, or rectally every + + + + + C 4 to 12 hours A wide variety of medications are used to treat vertigo and the frequently concurrent nausea and emesis.

evidence.title: Balance Problems | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: Have you ever felt dizzy , lightheaded, or as if the room is spinning around you? If the feeling happens often, it could be a sign of a balance problem. Balance problems can make you feel unsteady. You may also have blurred vision, confusion, and disorientation. They are one cause of falls and fall-related injuries, such as a hip fracture (broken hip).

evidence.title: Dizziness: Evaluation and Management | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Meniere disease is an inner ear disorder with multifactorial etiology that presents with recurrent and spontaneous episodes of vertigo, including hearing loss, tinnitus, and unidirectional, horizontal nystagmus. 29 Meniere disease has a biphasic occurrence affecting patients in their 20s and 60s. 30 The underlying pathology is hypothesized to be pressure from excess endolymphatic fluid, leading to inner ear dysfunction. 29 Clinicians should order an audiogram when assessing a patient for Meniere disease. 3 Conventionally, treatment includes limiting patients to less than 2,000 mg of salt per day, reducing caffeine intake, and limiting alcohol to one drink per day; however, this guidance is not supported by evidence. 31 Evidence is insufficient to recommend treating patients with diuretics or transtympanic injections of gentamicin or glucocorticoids. 11 , 29

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female who reports feeling dizzy for the past two days. She describes the dizziness as a sensation of the room spinning, consistent with vertigo. She denies hearing loss, ringing in the ears, or recent head injury. She has nausea without vomiting and no recent upper respiratory infection but has had a very runny nose and frequent nose blowing. The dizziness worsened last night after blowing her nose. She denies double vision, difficulty speaking, weakness, numbness, or difficulty walking. She has a history of migraine headaches but none recently and has had similar dizziness episodes every 3 to 4 months. No new medications or changes in medications. No recent ear pain or signs of infection except for chills last night without fever.

O: Phone encounter only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is peripheral vertigo, likely related to a benign vestibular cause possibly triggered by nasal congestion and pressure changes from frequent nose blowing.
Differential:
1. Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV)
2. Vestibular neuritis or labyrinthitis
3. Migraine-associated vertigo
4. Central nervous system pathology (less likely given no focal neurological symptoms)

P: - First-line medication: Meclizine 25 mg orally three times daily for 3 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Dimenhydrinate 50 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for dizziness (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: No known allergies, no pregnancy, no renal or liver disease; monitor for sedation or anticholinergic side effects
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if new neurological symptoms develop such as weakness, numbness, difficulty speaking, or severe headache; seek emergency care if dizziness worsens significantly or is accompanied by chest pain or shortness of breath
- Testing: Not indicated at this time based on telephone assessment; consider in-person evaluation if symptoms persist or worsen
